title: 36" Corner Base Pie-Cut Kitchen Cabinet
summary: A corner base cabinet with an angled pie-cut front that turns a dead kitchen corner into deep, reachable storage. Built to match a standard base run.
description:
The corner is where every kitchen wastes space, and a pie-cut cabinet is the honest fix — it fills the full corner and presents an angled door face so you can actually reach what's back there. This one is plywood casework designed to sit in a run of standard base cabinets, so the critical numbers aren't arbitrary: the sides are 34-1/2" tall by 22-1/4" wide with a 4-1/2" x 2-1/2" toe kick, matching the cabinets it lives beside. If your run uses different dimensions, change these to match yours first — a corner cabinet that doesn't align with its neighbors is obvious and permanent.

The smart material move here is mixing plywood grades: a good prefinished or veneer sheet where it shows, cheaper sheathing ply for the back and top that nobody sees. It's a real cost saver on a cabinet that eats a lot of sheet goods.

The face frame overhangs the sides by 1/4" on purpose, so it scribes cleanly against the adjacent cabinets. And note the pie-cut top is cut from an offcut of the back panel — plan your cuts so that scrap is big enough (at least 34" x 20") before you rip anything.

tools.note: Accurate sheet rips are what let a corner cabinet align with its neighbors.
For breaking sheets down before the table saw.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, cabinet grade
materials.note: Prefinished or good veneer for the visible sides.

materials.name: 3/4" plywood, sheathing grade
materials.note: Cheaper ply for the hidden back, top, and base supports.

materials.name: Hardwood 1x2, 8 ft
materials.note: Face frame — match the stiles to your other base cabinets.

materials.name: Cabinet door with concealed euro hinges
materials.note: Match height to the other base cabinets; width 3/8" wider than the face-frame opening.

cutList.note: Must match the side height/depth of your other base cabinets.
At least 34" x 20" to start; cut the angled pie-cut front from it.
Match the stiles from your other base cabinets.

steps.title: Confirm it matches your cabinet run
steps.body: Before cutting anything, confirm the governing dimensions match the base cabinets this will sit beside: a 34-1/2" tall by 22-1/4" wide side panel with a 4-1/2" x 2-1/2" toe kick is the standard here. If your run differs, adjust every part to match. A corner cabinet's whole job is to align with its neighbors.

steps.title: Attach the bottom with a 5" toe space
steps.body: Attach the bottom shelf leaving a 5" space beneath it — use the 5" wide plywood base-support pieces as spacers to line it up. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the underside of the side edges now, for attaching the sides next.

steps.title: Attach the sides
steps.body: Attach the sides to the back and bottom, keeping the 5" gap under the bottom shelf consistent. Drill 3/4" pocket holes along the front edges of the sides now for the face frame later — you won't be able to reach them once assembled.

steps.title: Cut and fit the pie-cut top
steps.body: Rather than buying a whole sheet for the top, use a scrap from the back — it must start at least 34" x 20". Cut the angled pie-cut front into it. Drill 3/4" pocket holes facing forward on the pie-cut front edges for attaching the angled face frame.

steps.title: Build and attach the face frame
steps.body: Build the face frame separately with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" pocket screws, and glue, then attach through the predrilled pocket holes. The face frame overextends the sides by 1/4" so it can scribe to the neighboring cabinets.

steps.title: Hang the door
steps.body: Hang the pie-cut door on concealed euro hinges. Match the door height to the other base cabinets; the door width should be 3/8" wider than the inside opening of the face frame for proper overlay.
materials: Cabinet door with concealed euro hinges

steps.title: Add wall blocking and finish
steps.body: Unlike the flat base cabinets, this corner has no 1/4" back to screw to the wall. Add scrap plywood blocking where you'll fasten the cabinet to the wall studs. Then sand and finish to match your kitchen.

images.alt: A corner kitchen base cabinet with an angled pie-cut door front
